claim: The Currier A/B split reflects two scribes encoding the same underlying language using two different cipher keys or glyph assignments, not two different languages or dialects, such that word-position glyph statistics are structurally identical across A and B but the specific glyph inventories differ.
evidence_for: Currier B (23,766 words) is roughly twice the size of A (11,022 words), consistent with one scribe copying from another's template rather than independent composition

Both A and B share the same word-initial glyph set {o,c,q,s,d} and word-final set {y,n,l,r,o}, suggesting the same positional grammar, which would be an improbable coincidence if two independent languages were used
Overall glyph entropy 3.8627 bits is uniform enough that two radically different encoding systems would likely produce measurable entropy divergence between sections
evidence_against: H004/H008 (confidence 0.808/0.742) favor genuine linguistic difference between A and B
If same key, transliteration between A and B word lists should reveal consistent glyph-to-glyph mappings, but no such mapping has been confirmed
Currier A and B show systematic differences in specific glyphs (e.g., 'a' rare in B), which is harder to explain by key-only variation
test: Extract bigram transition probability matrices P(g_j|g_i) separately for Currier A and Currier B. Compute chi-squared distance between the two matrices. Then generate 1000 random splits of the full corpus into same-sized A and B subsets and compute their chi-squared distances. Test whether observed A/B chi-squared distance falls within the 95th percentile of the random-split distribution.
test_metric: Chi-squared distance between Currier A and B glyph bigram transition matrices; p-value against random-split null distribution
test_threshold: p-value > 0.05 (A/B matrices not significantly different from random split) would support same-key hypothesis; p-value < 0.01 would support different language/cipher hypothesis

tests_run.test: extract bigram transition probability matrices p(g_j|g_i) separately for currier a and currier b. co
tests_run.details: Language A: entropy=3.8321, 3513 unique/11022 total words. Language B: entropy=3.8611, 5089 unique/23766 total words. Entropy difference: 0.0290. Vocabulary density difference: 0.1046.

reasoning: This test directly discriminates between 'same cipher, different key' and 'different language/cipher' by asking whether the internal glyph transition structure is statistically identical â€” a question answerable purely from corpus statistics without requiring decipherment.
